pr.title: [claude] add docs/glossary.md as the canonical term reference
pr.body:
## What

Adds `docs/glossary.md` — the canonical, one-sentence-per-term reference for every word other orgs-reins docs use. Grouped by context (the big picture, how Reins plugs in, how Reins decides, when something is blocked, record-keeping, for admins) so a non-technical reader skimming for "the word the agent just said at me" can find it without scrolling.

## Why

Both open PRs (`#1` when-blocked runbook, `#2` starter policy) ended up needing to define terms inline — PR #1 has a small embedded glossary at the bottom; PR #2's `policies/README.md` has its own. As soon as a third doc lands (`docs/onboarding.md`, `docs/admin-rollout.md`, the per-runtime sub-skills) that pattern starts duplicating definitions and inevitably drifting. A single canonical glossary lets every later doc link out for the long-form definition instead of redefining the term and risking contradiction.

It is also the doc with the lowest reading prerequisite in the whole repo: someone who has never installed Reins can still get value from it, which makes it a good entry point for the ops / compliance / IC audience this repo is for.

## Approach

Every term is grounded in a specific upstream file in [pegasi-ai/reins](https://github.com/pegasi-ai/reins) — `src/types.ts` for `Decision` and `SecurityRule`, `src/hooks/pre-tool-use.ts` for severity labels and the `exit 2` convention, `src/core/Arbitrator.ts` for the `CONFIRM-XXXXXX` token format (verified: SHA-1 of the action, first 6 hex chars uppercased), `src/core/Interceptor.ts` for `denyPaths` / `allowPaths` glob semantics, `src/config.ts` for the built-in `reads ALLOW / writes ASK / deletes DENY` defaults, `src/core/SecurityScanner.ts` for the scan check list, `src/cli/scan.ts` for drift detection, and the `plugin/skills/reins/SKILL.md` for the merge order. Every one of those links is verified to point at a real file in the upstream tree on `main`.

Two restraint calls worth flagging:

1. **"OWASP ASI10" is left unexpanded.** The upstream README, plugin description, and skill all use the acronym without expanding it. I'm not inventing an expansion (`Agent Security Initiative`? `Agentic Security Industry`?) when upstream chose not to. The glossary entry says so explicitly.
2. **Severity is described as both `HIGH / CRITICAL / CATASTROPHIC` (the source-of-truth labels in `pre-tool-use.ts`) and the `CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW` collapse that PR #1's runbook uses.** Both are accurate — the source ships three labels for blocked actions, and end-user UIs sometimes collapse them — so the entry flags this rather than picking one and contradicting the runbook.

## Non-goals for this PR

- No README pointer. Both `#1` and `#2` already add a "what's here today" line, and they will conflict with each other on merge — adding a third here would just compound the conflict. Once one merges, a follow-up PR can consolidate the section.
- No backlinks from `when-blocked.md` or `policies/README.md` into the glossary. Those PRs are still open and unmerged; touching their files here would create a merge dependency. Backlinks can be added in a small follow-up once #1 and #2 land.
- No per-runtime jargon (Hermes / OpenClaw / Replit Agent specifics). Those belong in the per-runtime sub-skills.

## Review checklist

- [ ] Every term is defined in one sentence a non-technical reader can follow.
- [ ] Every upstream link points to a file that actually exists on `pegasi-ai/reins@main` (I verified all 10 paths locally).
- [ ] No term contradicts PR #1's embedded mini-glossary or PR #2's `policies/README.md` glossary — same definitions, more depth.
- [ ] No invented Reins behavior (the only acronym I would not expand, ASI10, is explicitly left unexpanded).

Awaiting review before merge.

---
